findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the Mypy-pinning prerequisite story as a formal `depends_on` dep on ticket d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d so the ordering assumption stated in the Approach is enforced by the dependency graph, not just prose.
evidence: Story d9f5 Approach step 1: 'Depend on the approved dependency-freezing work to pin the canonical Mypy version and provision all project extras used by CI.'
d9f5 ticket deps field: [] (empty — no formal prerequisite link to the dependency-freezing story)
pyproject.toml line 169: 'mypy>=1.10' — a version floor, not a pin

Story d9f5 Context: 'Because unused-ignore results can change with Mypy, Python, dependency stubs, and optional extras, cleanup must be performed only in the pinned, fully provisioned CI environment.'
findings.finding: Story d9f5's approach states 'Depend on the approved dependency-freezing work to pin the canonical Mypy version' but the ticket's deps[] is empty (no formal prerequisite link). The plan acknowledges that warn_unused_ignores results can change across Mypy versions — without a hard dependency enforcing that the Mypy-pinning work lands first, the story can be claimed and executed in an environment with any Mypy >=1.10, causing the 23-diagnostic baseline to shift unexpectedly and producing either spurious cleanup or missed suppressions.
scenarios: An implementer claims d9f5 before the Mypy-pinning work is complete; they run against Mypy 2.4.x (a newer patch) where a stub update eliminates 3 of the 23 unused-ignores, so they delete those suppressions — but on the pinned 2.3.0 CI environment those suppressions are still needed, causing CI red.
The dependency-freezing work is never linked, so a future reviewer of the epic cannot verify the prerequisite is satisfied before claiming d9f5.
findings.suggested_fix: In the d9f5 ticket, add the dependency-freezing story's ticket ID to the deps[] list (e.g. `rebar dep add d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d depends_on <pinning-story-id>`). If that work is already merged, add a comment recording the Mypy version in use and remove the conditional language from step 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add the dependency-freezing work as a named prerequisite in the parent epic (with a blocking dependency link) or document the specific Mypy version and extras-provisioning requirement as a formal entry condition in d9f5's Dependencies section.
evidence: d9f5 Approach step 1: 'Depend on the approved dependency-freezing work to pin the canonical Mypy version and provision all project extras used by CI.'
The parent's Child Decomposition lists only four children; no dependency-freezing story appears in the sibling roster.
d9f5 explicitly warns: 'Newly surfaced diagnostics are recorded as discovered work rather than silently expanding this story' — acknowledging environment sensitivity.
The parent's ordering section ('d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d' is first) does not name any prerequisite outside the four children.
container pairing: parent + 125d-ee6f-7af0-4200/28cd-d8f8-4456-482c/c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77/d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d
findings.finding: Child d9f5 declares a prerequisite on 'approved dependency-freezing work to pin the canonical Mypy version and provision all project extras' that is neither a sibling nor tracked anywhere in the parent epic, creating an untracked external dependency gap.
findings.location: child d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d — Approach step 1 and implicit throughout the warn_unused_ignores cleanup
scenarios: The dependency-freezing work is not yet complete when d9f5 is scheduled; the implementer runs warn_unused_ignores in a partially provisioned environment, removes ignores that are still needed under full provisioning, and introduces Mypy failures in the integrated CI environment.

The dependency-freezing work ships with a different pinned Mypy version than 2.3.0 (the baseline documented in d9f5); the 23-diagnostic baseline count changes, making the documented line numbers stale and the cleanup incomplete or over-aggressive.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit entry condition to d9f5: 'Entry condition: the canonical CI environment pins Mypy 2.3.0 with all project extras installed. Verify with pip show mypy before beginning cleanup.' Also add a blocking prerequisite link from d9f5 to the dependency-freezing ticket in the parent's Approach ordering.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'make lint exits 0' to d9f5's Testing/Verification commands and Acceptance Criteria.
evidence: d9f5 AC lists: make typecheck exits 0, mypy standalone commands, pytest tiers — but not make lint.
28cd AC explicitly includes: 'make lint exits 0'
125d AC explicitly includes: 'make lint exits 0'
c9f7 AC explicitly includes: 'make lint invokes the check-only complexity ratchet and remains green'
d9f5 Scope includes pyproject.toml edits (Mypy settings) and 23 source files with noqa removal — changes that affect Ruff's RUF100 stale-suppression check.
Parent AC2: 'make lint exits 0 from the integrated feature worktree' — d9f5 provides no child-level coverage of this for its own changes.
container pairing: parent + 125d-ee6f-7af0-4200/28cd-d8f8-4456-482c/c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77/d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d
findings.finding: Child d9f5 modifies pyproject.toml and multiple source files but does not include 'make lint exits 0' in its Testing/Verification or Acceptance Criteria, leaving its own changes unvalidated against the Ruff-gated lint gate that every other pyproject.toml-editing sibling verifies.
findings.location: child d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d — Testing/Verification and Acceptance Criteria sections
scenarios: d9f5 removes a noqa comment from one of the 23 listed files; the underlying Ruff rule now fires on that line (because the rule was still needed). make lint would catch this, but since d9f5 doesn't run it, the failure appears only when 28cd or 125d runs make lint on the integrated branch, making diagnosis harder.
d9f5 edits pyproject.toml and inadvertently introduces a Ruff-invalid configuration key; make lint catches it, but not in d9f5's own verification pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Insert 'make lint exits 0' between the standalone Mypy commands and make typecheck in d9f5's Testing/Verification section, and add a corresponding AC item mirroring the pattern used by its siblings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'pytest -m not integration and not external -q exits 0' to c9f7's Testing section and Acceptance Criteria.
evidence: c9f7 Testing section lists: pytest tests/unit/test_complexity_baseline.py (focused) and make lint — no full default-tier pytest run.
c9f7 AC items: focused unit test, check command exit 0, make lint, checker help — no pytest -m 'not integration and not external' -q.
Parent AC5: 'pytest -m not integration and not external -q exits 0 from the integrated feature worktree.'
128cd, 125d, and d9f5 each include the full default-tier pytest run in their own AC.
c9f7 adds scripts/check_complexity_baseline.py and tests/unit/test_complexity_baseline.py — new test infrastructure that could interact with pytest collection and existing fixtures.
container pairing: parent + 125d-ee6f-7af0-4200/28cd-d8f8-4456-482c/c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77/d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d
findings.finding: Child c9f7 does not include 'pytest -m not integration and not external -q exits 0' in its Acceptance Criteria or Testing section, leaving the parent's default-tier regression criterion (AC5) without child-level coverage for c9f7's Makefile and scripts changes.
findings.location: child c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77 — Testing and Acceptance Criteria sections
scenarios: c9f7 introduces a new test file (test_complexity_baseline.py) that has a collection error or import side-effect causing unrelated tests in the default tier to fail; without running the full default tier, this goes undetected until the integrated worktree run.

The complexity checker script imports a module that conflicts with an existing test fixture or conftest.py setup; the full default-tier run would catch it, but c9f7's focused-only verification misses it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a final AC item to c9f7: '[ ] pytest -m "not integration and not external" -q exits 0' to match the verification pattern of all sibling stories and provide child-level coverage of the parent's AC5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'next-batch' in the Approach section — state what tool or command it refers to and what output constitutes 'no file-impact conflict', or remove the gate if it is not needed.
evidence: 'next-batch' appears once in the plan with no definition, no link, and no surrounding context that would make it inferrable
The complexity story precondition is gated on this undefined check
findings.finding: The phrase 'when next-batch reports no file-impact conflict' in the Approach section uses the term 'next-batch' without defining what it refers to — a tool, a CI pipeline stage, a scheduled job, or something else. An executing agent cannot determine when the complexity story (c9f7) is unblocked without knowing what 'next-batch' is.
findings.location: Approach section: 'The complexity story does not share pyproject.toml and may proceed independently when next-batch reports no file-impact conflict.'
scenarios: An agent starts the complexity story immediately because it cannot identify the 'next-batch' gate
An agent stalls indefinitely waiting for a condition it cannot observe
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'when next-batch reports no file-impact conflict' with a concrete check, e.g., 'when `rebar next-batch --output json` reports no overlapping file paths with the pyproject.toml stories' or simply remove the gate and note that c9f7 may proceed at any time since it does not touch pyproject.toml.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note in the plan body (Approach or Verification Checkpoints) explaining what 'config-check' validates, which story (or the integration worktree step) is responsible for ensuring it passes, and whether it is a pre-existing project-standard gate or new work.
evidence: AC line: 'config-check exits 0 from the integrated feature worktree'
ABSENCE: the term 'config-check' does not appear in Context, Approach, Child Decomposition, or Verification Checkpoints sections
Three child stories (d9f5, 28cd, 125d) edit pyproject.toml but none mention verifying config-check
findings.finding: The AC 'config-check exits 0 from the integrated feature worktree' references a command ('config-check') that appears nowhere in the plan body — no story describes work to make config-check pass, no Approach section explains what it validates, and no child ticket is assigned ownership of it. If pyproject.toml edits can break config-check, the stories touching that file should explicitly verify it; if it is a pre-existing project gate, the plan should say so.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'config-check exits 0 from the integrated feature worktree'
scenarios: An implementer completes all four stories, sees make lint and make typecheck pass, then discovers config-check fails because a pyproject.toml edit broke a validation the plan never mentioned — no story owns the fix.
A reviewer cannot trace the config-check AC back to any described deliverable, leaving it unmeasurable.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Verification Checkpoints section, add: 'config-check is a pre-existing project gate that validates pyproject.toml schema/tool config consistency; each story that edits pyproject.toml must run it locally before submitting. No new work is required beyond keeping it green.' If it is not pre-existing, assign ownership to one of the pyproject.toml-editing stories.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan to the Approach section: e.g., 'If integration verification fails, revert the offending story's pyproject.toml hunk via `git revert <commit>` on the feature branch and re-run verification before re-attempting merge to main.'
evidence: 'Deliver independently reviewed stories into a short-lived Gerrit feature branch, then merge the completed branch atomically to main.'
No section describes what to do if 'make lint exits 0 from the integrated feature worktree' fails after all children have merged to the feature branch.
The plan explicitly values rollback ('Rejected: one large cleanup change ... make rollback difficult') but does not apply that reasoning to the epic's own feature branch.
findings.finding: The plan lacks an explicit back-out/rollback procedure for the feature branch. If integration verification fails after all children merge to the feature branch, there is no stated mechanism to revert the pyproject.toml changes or unwind the branch — the T4 criterion requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not just an atomic-merge strategy.
findings.location: Approach section / Verification Checkpoints / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Integration verification reveals a cross-story conflict (e.g., a RUF rule enabled in 28cd conflicts with a pattern fixed in 125d) — the team has no stated procedure to isolate and revert the offending change on the feature branch.
A Mypy setting enabled in d9f5 surfaces a latent type error introduced by 28cd's timezone fixes — no rollback path is specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection under Approach: 'If any integration-gate command fails after all children have merged to the feature branch, identify the offending story by bisecting the feature branch, `git revert` its pyproject.toml commit, push a fixup to Gerrit for re-review, and re-run the full Acceptance Criteria suite before retrying the merge to main.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section documenting the shrink-only ratchet pattern, the C901 threshold choice, and the rationale for each deferred rule (disallow_any_generics, disallow_untyped_defs, S-family, Semgrep).
evidence: 'add a symbol-level shrink-only C901 baseline at threshold 15' — novel ratchet pattern introduced with no doc artifact
'explicitly defers disallow_any_generics, disallow_untyped_defs, Ruff security-family S rules, and Semgrep expansion' — architectural decisions with no named ADR or AGENTS.md update
ABSENCE: no mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel 'shrink-only symbol-level ratchet' pattern for C901 complexity enforcement and makes explicit architectural deferral decisions (disallow_any_generics, disallow_untyped_defs, Ruff S-family, Semgrep expansion) but names no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc update to capture these decisions for future maintainers.
findings.location: Approach section / Child Decomposition (c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77) / Context paragraph on deferrals
scenarios: A future engineer sees C901 threshold=15 in pyproject.toml and doesn't know it's a ratchet (shrink-only) or why 15 was chosen.
A future engineer re-evaluates enabling S-family rules and has no record of why they were deferred, leading to duplicate analysis effort.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (e.g., docs/adr/0042-quality-gate-ratchet.md) or an AGENTS.md section titled 'Quality Gate Ratchet Strategy' that records: (1) the shrink-only rule for C901 and its initial threshold, (2) the four Mypy flags enabled and why the remaining two are deferred, (3) the Ruff rule families enabled vs. deferred with one-line rationale each.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In c9f7, verify and document how function names are extracted from `ruff --output-format json` C901 output (confirm the message-field parsing approach or a structured alternative), and add a test fixture with a sample Ruff JSON payload to pin the extraction logic against format changes.
evidence: c9f7 ticket Approach: 'consumes Ruff JSON at max-complexity 15 and compares findings to a checked-in baseline keyed by path and function with the current score'
c9f7 ticket AC: 'The current 52 findings are represented by exact symbol-level ceilings without per-file or inline C901 ignores'

Ruff's --output-format json for C901 embeds the function name only in the message string (e.g. "Function 'foo' is too complex (16 > 15) [C901]"), not as a dedicated structured field — plan does not acknowledge or validate this parsing dependency
ABSENCE: no plan text or AC describes how the function name will be extracted from the Ruff JSON message field, nor does any story verify this with a sample Ruff JSON fixture
findings.finding: The c9f7 child story asserts the complexity baseline will be keyed by 'path and function with the current score,' implying per-symbol function names are extractable from Ruff JSON output. However, Ruff's `--output-format json` for C901 violations does not expose the function name as a structured field — the function name appears only in the human-readable `message` string (e.g., `"Function 'foo' is too complex (16 > 15) [C901]"`). The plan does not verify or document this extraction mechanism, making it an unverified capability assertion load-bearing for the baseline script's correctness.
findings.location: Child Decomposition — c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77 (story ticket, Approach section): 'keyed by path and function with the current score'
scenarios: If Ruff changes its C901 message format in a future pinned-version bump, the baseline script silently fails to match function names, causing false 'new finding' alerts or masking real regressions.
If the function name extraction uses fragile string parsing (e.g., regex on 'Function \'%s\' is too complex'), test coverage of the extractor may be insufficient to catch format drift.
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/check_complexity_baseline.py`, parse the C901 function name from the `message` field using the known Ruff format (`r"Function '(.+)' is too complex"`), document this dependency explicitly in the script's help text, and add a unit test in `tests/unit/test_complexity_baseline.py` that exercises the parser with a hard-coded Ruff JSON fixture — so a message-format change surfaces as a test failure rather than a silent miscount.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the first AC bullet: `rebar list-descendants` always outputs JSON and takes no `--output` flag — remove `--output json` from the command, and clarify how 'no open or in_progress direct child' is verified from the bucketed-by-type output (e.g., by piping through a status-check step or using a different rebar command that exposes child status).
evidence: tests/golden/cli_help/list-descendants__help.out line 1: `Usage: rebar list-descendants <root_ticket_id>   (BFS walk bucketed by type, JSON)` — no --output flag documented
src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py lines 77-85: `list_descendants_cli` parses only `argv[0]` as ticket ID; no flag parsing present
docs/cli-reference.md line 260: signature shown without --output flag
Evidence class: Contradicted — repo source directly falsifies the --output flag claim
findings.finding: The AC invokes `rebar list-descendants ... --output json` but the `list-descendants` subcommand accepts no `--output` flag; it always emits JSON. Passing an unknown flag will cause a runtime error or be silently mis-handled, making the AC unexecutable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first AC bullet: `rebar list-descendants facb-44c3-2ada-41ee --output json reports no open or in_progress direct child`
scenarios: When an implementer runs the literal AC command, the `--output` token will either be treated as the ticket ID argument (producing a wrong-ID lookup) or passed into the implementation where there is no parser for it, causing the AC check to silently use the wrong input or fail with a usage error.
Automated CI verification that runs ACs verbatim will fail at this step, blocking the epic's close gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC bullet with a two-step verification: `rebar list-descendants facb-44c3-2ada-41ee` (emits JSON with child IDs by type), then verify each returned ID has status `closed` via `rebar show <id>` — or, if a single-command form is preferred, use `rebar quality-check facb-44c3-2ada-41ee --output json` if that surfaces child completion status. Alternatively confirm whether `rebar next-batch facb-44c3-2ada-41ee --output json` returning an empty batch is the intended idiom.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact JSON schema for `.github/complexity-baseline.json` in c9f7 — specify the key format (e.g., `"path/to/file.py::function_name"`), value type (integer score), and a concrete example entry — so the checker script and tests implement a compatible contract.
evidence: c9f7 Approach: 'a checked-in baseline keyed by path and function with the current score'
c9f7 Approach: 'Unlisted findings, increased scores, duplicate keys, malformed baseline data, or missing Ruff fail the gate'
AC: 'python scripts/check_complexity_baseline.py --check exiting zero and printing active=52 new=0 increased=0 stale=0'
ABSENCE: no JSON schema, no example entry, no key-format specification anywhere in the epic or c9f7 story
findings.finding: The `.github/complexity-baseline.json` schema — the key format and value structure — is never defined. The c9f7 story says the file is 'keyed by path and function with the current score' but does not specify the exact key format (e.g., flat string 'src/rebar/foo.py:my_func', nested dict, separator for class methods), the value type (bare integer, dict with a 'score' field, etc.), or what a 'malformed' entry looks like. Both `scripts/check_complexity_baseline.py` and `tests/unit/test_complexity_baseline.py` must implement against this schema, and duplicate-key detection is explicitly listed as a gate — making the exact structure load-bearing.
findings.location: c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77 Approach / Scope / AC; epic file_impact `.github/complexity-baseline.json`
scenarios: Executor writing check_complexity_baseline.py uses a nested dict keyed by path then function name; executor writing test_complexity_baseline.py uses a flat 'path::func' string key — duplicate-key test passes for one format but would silently miss real duplicates under the other.
Class-method complexity entries (e.g., MyClass.my_method) have no defined canonical key form, so the 52-entry baseline could be populated inconsistently.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to c9f7 Approach: 'The baseline is a flat JSON object. Each key is `<relative-path>::<qualified-function-name>` (e.g., `"src/rebar/foo.py::MyClass.method"`); each value is the integer complexity score at the time of baseline capture. An entry is malformed if its value is not an integer or its key does not match the `<path>::<name>` pattern.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'next-batch' in the epic Approach — specify what command to run, what output or exit code signals 'no file-impact conflict', or replace the reference with a concrete check (e.g., 'confirm no other open story has `Makefile` or `pyproject.toml` in its file_impact').
evidence: Epic Approach: 'The complexity story does not share pyproject.toml and may proceed independently when next-batch reports no file-impact conflict.'
ABSENCE: 'next-batch' is not defined, explained, or linked in any of the five tickets (epic, c9f7, d9f5, 28cd, 125d).
ABSENCE: no file_impact entry, no verify_command, no scope item, and no AC references 'next-batch' in any child ticket.
findings.finding: The epic Approach references 'next-batch' as a gating concept for the complexity story's independent parallel execution ('may proceed independently when next-batch reports no file-impact conflict'), but 'next-batch' is never defined anywhere in the plan or child tickets — its interface, invocation, and output format are entirely unspecified. An executor cannot evaluate this condition.
findings.location: Epic Approach section: 'The complexity story does not share pyproject.toml and may proceed independently when next-batch reports no file-impact conflict.'
scenarios: An executor claiming c9f7 does not know whether 'next-batch' is a CLI tool, a rebar sub-command, a CI job name, or a manual check — and therefore cannot determine whether the independence precondition is satisfied before starting.
If 'next-batch' is a rebar scheduling tool that checks for file-impact conflicts, its output format (how it signals 'no conflict') is undefined, making the gate unevaluable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add config-check to the Testing/Verification and Acceptance Criteria of at least one sequenced pyproject.toml-editing child (d9f5, 28cd, or 125d) so the parent AC is covered before the final integration worktree run.
evidence: Parent AC explicitly lists: 'config-check exits 0 from the integrated feature worktree'
125d AC: no mention of config-check
28cd AC: no mention of config-check
c9f7 AC: no mention of config-check
d9f5 AC: no mention of config-check
Cross-checked against the complete sibling roster — no sibling covers this criterion
container pairing: parent + 125d-ee6f-7af0-4200/28cd-d8f8-4456-482c/c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77/d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d
findings.finding: Parent acceptance criterion 'config-check exits 0 from the integrated feature worktree' is uncovered by any child. No child's Testing/Verification section or Acceptance Criteria mentions config-check, leaving this parent AC with zero child-level verification coverage.
findings.location: Parent AC: 'config-check exits 0 from the integrated feature worktree' — absent from all four children (125d-ee6f-7af0-4200, 28cd-d8f8-4456-482c, c9f7-d6df-bce0-4b77, d9f5-baa5-9fc3-451d)
scenarios: A pyproject.toml edit from d9f5, 28cd, or 125d introduces a syntax error or invalid configuration key; config-check would catch this but it is never run during any child's verification, so the integrated worktree could fail at the parent's post-merge gate with no earlier signal.
The integrated worktree's first run of config-check is the final merge-back step, leaving no opportunity to diagnose and fix the failure within the feature branch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'config-check exits 0' to d9f5's AC (as it is the first pyproject.toml editor) and to the integrated verification step in 28cd and 125d, so each sequential editor confirms the configuration remains valid after its edits.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
